All right, so now we are on the final piece of retention, the final horseman exit interviews. Okay, so you got to the point where your retention efforts haven't haven't gone through with everybody. So there are some people that are going to want to cancel. And then so here's what we do when they do reach out to you and say, Hey, I'd like to cancel my membership. We have a system for that. And let's cover it now. Key factors are going to be that these are all done in person. Really important to make sure that this is not just being emailed to somebody. It gives us a chance to reconnect with the client. It may have been quite some time since you sat down and actually talked with them. We're going to refocus on why they started in the first place. So it might not have been too long since you sold them. They sat down in your office and you guys talked about all of their goals and what they want to accomplish. We need to identify if that changed. If they've reached their goals, if not, and why wouldn't they want to continue reaching those goals? Then we want to create some FOMO, some fear of missing out. So we want to explain to them that even if they do leave, it might not be the exact same situation that they're in right now when they want to come back. And finally, we want to save the client by setting a 30-day goal to revisit. So we'll set up a short-term goal and give it 110% and revisit their decision in 30 days and see if they still want to cancel. So let's dig into the first one, in-person. So this is probably the most important piece of the process. Don't just send them a form, an exit interview form. We'll be going over the exit interview form, but we don't want to just email that to them. This should be an in-person consultation. Think of it like a sale. You're selling them on why they should continue with the program. So instead, send them a link. And that link is a 15-minute member meeting with you, so an acuity link, or whatever online calendar you're using. Send them a link and say, hey, let's schedule a quick meeting. We'll sit down. We'll talk about your membership. We'll process the cancellation, and we'll just make sure that we did everything we could to help you. And this is where you'll go over the form together. And if they still wish to cancel, then you can process it for them yourself. So that's kind of how you position the fact that you do want to meet with them and make sure you did everything you could to help them. But if not, you'll take care of it yourself. So if possible, put this process into the cancellation part of your contracts. So add this into your contracts. If at any point you wish to cancel, a quick 15-minute meeting is required with so-and-so to discuss your program, however you want to word it. But if you can, I would start putting that into the contract. It doesn't have to be in big and bold letters. But simply enforcing this policy will decrease churn because a lot of people just don't want to go through the effort. There's a lot of people that don't cancel all sorts of memberships because they don't want to just do it. They don't want to have the meeting. They don't want to go through the effort. That's not the point of this. But as a byproduct of having to do effort, some people just won't. They just won't cancel. They'll be like, all right, I'm going to keep pushing through. It also gives you a chance to use more persuasion than just words, like an email. So you could literally email them the exact same thing as you would say. But in person, you can use things like tonality, body language, different persuasion techniques like you use in sales to get them to rethink their decision to not want to train at your gym anymore. So this is really important when selling, which at this point you are doing. You're selling them on why they should stay. So we want to treat this like a sale. Now if they do not want to meet in person, you'll have to use your judgment as to why. And if it's a valid reason or if it's acceptable for your contract. So if they say like, hey, I don't want to meet with you. I just want to cancel, you got to kind of figure out why that is. And if you want to let it go, or if you want to continue pushing it. What we do is typically we make a few efforts to get them to meet in person. We offer a bunch of times that are convenient just based on when they normally come into the gym to work out and say, hey, look, it looks like you train every single Wednesday at 5pm. Let's meet at 545 right after class. Typically if you go out of your way to make it super convenient to somebody, they're not going to say no. If you do have somebody that is just super adamant about not meeting in person, that's when you want to take a look at your contract and say, look, do I have this clause in my contract or not? If you don't have it in there, you might just have to let them cancel or try to do the exit interview over email or text or some way. I would still try to do it the way that it's set up in here, but maybe just not in person. So even zoom, like you could use zoom. You could say, hey, why don't you just click on this link and just give them your zoom link and say, well, chat really quick. And I want to get a good idea of where we failed you, basically. So for current members who don't have this written into their contract, which a lot of them won't if this is the first time you're going through this, you can explain that this is your new process. But ultimately, you have to comply with the terms of your agreement with them. So you can say, hey, this is our new process. But if they never signed anything that says that they have to do this, then you have to just comply with, you have to let them cancel if that's what's in your current agreement now. So let's go into reconnecting with them. Take the time to ask how things are going with your client. So don't just get right down to business and start filling out a form like you're doing some kind of formality. Build a rapport with them. Spend the first five minutes just listening and getting to understand where they're at and how their life's going and all of that stuff before you dig into the questions. That's going to be huge in any sale, is building a little, letting their guard down a little bit. So try to get them to laugh, let their guard down. This will make it an easier path to getting to the root cause as to why they're looking to cancel. And hopefully you can change your mind. So everybody has a reason. Everybody came to that conclusion for somehow is at one point they say, you know what? I'm going to cancel this gym membership. You have to figure out why that is and the actual reason why. Not some, well, you know, I've been busy. Everybody's busy. Why do they value other things over coming to the gym? Why are they not prioritizing their health and fitness? That's your job to figure out the root cause. And once you identify that, that's when you can find a true solution that makes sense. If you can't get to the root cause, any solution you give them, they're not going to buy into it because it's not solving their real problem. Right? So you need to find the real problem so you can give them a real solution to that and they might think about it and they're like, you know what? That is, that might work. And then you save that client. So let's get that, let's focus on the why. Why did they sign up with you in the first place? So this in-person meeting is an opportunity to revisit why they joined your gym in the first place. The exit interview will help you walk through the conversation. Right? So number one, did they reach their goals? Are they canceling because they reached the goal that they set out for and they no longer have that goal? Right? Do they still have the goal? Do you think that they dedicated themselves enough to fitness, nutrition and accountability? If they did that, if they did dedicate themselves to those things, enough, do you think they would reach those goals? Right? And maybe that's a conversation where they say, look, I just don't feel like no matter how hard I work with this current program, I'm not going to reach my goals. Right? And that is something that, that's when you need to look at the fulfillment if you really do believe them. Now, if you don't think that they're actually following the program, that's a chance for you to say, well, we've had a ton of people that have success with this program. What does your nutrition look like? Have you been tracking? Have you been following this? Have you been doing X, Y and Z? And if they say, well, I really haven't been doing that, or you can look right into your CRM and say, Hey, Mrs. Jones, it looks like you've only done two workouts this week. Typically, our members are at three times a week minimum. Like you're not burning as many calories as everyone else is, or that combined with you are not eating breakfast or whatever it may be. Like, do you think if you did those things, then you would reach that initial goal that you had? And if they're like, yeah, you know, if I really did those and say, well, okay, so what's changed? You know, you still have the exact same goal as you had when you first came in here, and it came down to an execution issue and not an issue with the program itself. So let's take a look at the execution. Let's revisit that. How can we help you execute better? And then you're pivoting the conversation away from, I want to cancel my gym membership to what happens? Do I need to change in my life to make sure that I have this healthy lifestyle? So that's a great way to pivot away from their initial thought. And now you're really talking about something completely different. So if they do still have the original goal and they do think your program can help them get there, that's a really good path to avoid that cancellation. This is probably the most important part of the conversation you're having with them. If they have not reached their goals, there's still a gap. There's still this gap, a gap between where they would like to be and where they're at right now. And you are still that bridge. Maybe they just need to be reminded of that. So you need to, originally when you sold them, you had them identify with the fact that they have a problem. You need to re-identify that problem with them so they associate themselves with having that problem. Mrs. Jones, do you still want to lose weight? Yes. Okay. Then you still have a problem of weight loss. And we are still that solution. We just need to come up with a new plan on how we can overcome that. So this is a really important piece of the conversation is helping identify the fact that they're not finished yet. And whether they are at your gym or they leave and they're not at your gym, they still have a problem that needs solving and you're still that solution. So you need to show them exactly how you can get them there and don't let them give up on themselves. And if you seem pushy in the conversation, if you seem like you're really trying, that's why they hired you in the first place. That's why they hired a coach to help them not quit. They've been quitting on themselves however long and to the point where they needed assistance, eating healthy, getting in shape, working out, holding themselves accountable, that hasn't changed and you haven't changed. You're still their coach, which is why you're going to not let them give up on themselves. And that's why you do these exit interviews is because it's true to your values as a gym. So after that discussion, you're going to get into the fear of missing out. So with each one of these, let's say you recovered them after that last conversation, talking about their why and do they still have these problems and you get to the point of like, okay, that's what I thought. Well, we do have the solution. Let's get back on the track. If you recover them after that, which is basically them saying, all right, I don't want to cancel, let's create a plan. You don't have to go into the fear of missing out. However, if they say, yeah, I hear you, but I, you know, I still want to take a break. I still want to take some time to try it on my own. I want to see, right? That's when you hit them with the fear of missing out, right? So if they're still on the fence after reassessing their goals, this is where you can sway their decision in your favor, create scarcity and urgency. All right. So examples, if they leave, you can't make any guarantees that they can work with the same coach when they come back. So if you have your accountability coaches broken up into blocks and they've built really good rapport with some of the coaches that they're working with or the one that they've been communicating with, like you can guarantee that all of a sudden three months later, when they decide or they realize they can't do it on their own, that they get to come back to the exact same coach because maybe that coach is full. Maybe they're not getting any new, they're not in the rotation anymore. They're not getting any new clients. They're not taking anybody else. So they're going to get whoever the new coach is or however you break it up. People don't like change. They don't like that. Secondly, you can say something like, hey, if you leave, just you know, like your grandfathered in at a special rate. And I think this is true for a lot of gyms because, you know, somebody might have gotten a deal where they're paying $149 a month for unlimited or three times a week. And now you're charging $49 a week and maybe you didn't, you know, enforce that change on all of your current members. If that's the case, they're getting it significantly cheaper and you can't guarantee that rate when they come back. Even though you'd like to, it's the way that you explain it is it wouldn't be fair to the clients that are signing up now. It's, it's a grandfathered rate, but it's not a discount for the rest of your life. Right. So there's no, there's no determining factor of how long that discount would be good for, right. And that's, and they'll understand that that's true across all businesses. Like if you cancel your cell phone contract and you want to come back a year later, they're not, they're not going to give, they're going to give it to you, whatever you, whatever they're selling to that. And your gym's the same. Right. So, um, that is something that you might want to bring it up. And, uh, you'll tell them, Hey, I'm just letting you know this because 75% of people who leave end up rejoining, right? Within six months, um, when they see that there's nothing else really like this. And you could say, Mrs. Jones, have you ever been to another gym like ours? And they'll be like, no, right? Because even though there's other micro gyms or whatever, nothing's really like yours. Say exactly. Right. And we've had a lot of members that have been in this office, having the same conversation with me and they wanted to try it on their own as well. Um, and so we let them, we did, you know, and all of a sudden they came back three months later and they didn't have the same coach and they did have to pay our new rates because we believe in doing what's fair. Right. And so it ends up costing them a lot of time and money. Um, so I just wanted to shed some light on that for you based on our prior members experience, like you're doing, you're doing them a favor. You're doing your, uh, you would be doing them a disservice if you didn't mention this to them, the fact that this is what normally happens, that people might, you know, want to see, uh, if they can do it on their own or a different approach. Um, but they always end up regretting it because you have to stay true to your new members that includes them, which they would be at that point as a new member. So, um, don't come off as like threatening like, well, you know, if you leave, you know, don't, don't come off as threatening or giving them an ultimatum. Right. Um, this will have the opposite effect. That you're looking for and they'll probably get mad. You know, if they feel like you're trying to bully them, um, that's not going to work and they probably won't come back either. Uh, it just turns the conversation in a way that isn't, isn't productive for either side. Right. So just simply talk to them like a friend who's trying to help them make the best decision they can, literally put yourself in the, in the, in the mind frame of, I'm this person's best friend and they have this problem. And I'm going to try to tell, I'm going to talk with them about what the real best solution is. And if you have that mindset, um, they'll, they'll understand that you're coming from a place of really wanting to help. And, uh, and they'll, they'll realize that sincerity and hopefully, um, they'll realize that you, you do, you still do really want to help them. And so, uh, the best way that you can help them is by them, you know, continuing to work out and not giving up on themselves. So, um, if that works, if, if they are now, uh, if the fear of missing out did not quite push them all the way to saying, all right, I'm going to keep going. We have a final approach that is set a 30 day action plan. Okay. So if they're still in the fence after a little bit of FOMO and they're like, yeah, I guess I don't want to lose the rates, but you know what? Like, I think I can swing another $20 a week or whatever. This is when you hit them with a, you offer them to create a custom 30 day action plan, uh, and you'll set goals for them for the next 30 days. And you do this right in the exit interview. Like it's okay if it goes a little, if it goes 15 more minutes over, say, look, we're going to set goals right now for the next 30 days. Don't set a follow up appointment to set goals that just gives them time to rethink about it again. Say right now we're going to set goals for the next 30 days. We're going to identify what you're going to do for nutrition. Like what have you done in the past with us that you really liked and maybe what's some of the stuff that you didn't work so well for you. Cause we're going to pick the ones that you really liked. We're going to take a look at your history of workouts and make sure that you don't miss any. So we're going to set a goal for 30 days, right? If they follow the nutrition program, um, they're going to check in with their coach and they don't skip any workouts, right? If they do this, uh, for the next 30 days and still, still don't hit their goals, you'll cancel their membership and refund them for the month. Right. That's what you're telling them. So what actually happens is they try really hard. They get amazing goals and whether or not they hit the goal, they're going to stay anyhow. Right. But even if you do have to refund somebody, it's money that you wouldn't have had anyways, cause they're in your office to cancel. Right. So at least you're delaying it for 30 days and you're giving yourself a high, a high percentage shot to keep them. That's the way that you should look at it is like they came to your office to cancel and fill out a form. So worst case scenario, you push it off 30 days and maybe they're going to cancel and maybe they're not right. And that's, that's a best case solution, but they'd also have to cancel after they gave 110%. Right. That's the deal you're making with them is in, and the reason that you do this is it takes away all the risk of losing money. Um, and, and, and gives the gym another chance to keep them as a member. So the next 30 days is on the house. Right. Unless they hit the goals, then they never canceled. Right. But if they didn't hit the goals or they still want to cancel afterwards, which if you set a goal of 10 pounds and Mrs. Jones loses five pounds and she feels great, she's not going to cancel. Right. So it's not always about the goals, but you want, it's, it's one of those like money back guarantee satisfaction things. Right. Um, so remember they came into cancel this delays the decision and gives you a chance to revisit it in 30 days when they will most likely be in a better state of mind. Right. They'll feel great. They'll, they'll be energized. They just gave 110%. Having them agree to this is considered a recovery. If they say, you know what, I'll do this. Whenever they walk out of your office and they didn't cancel, whether you hit, whether you got them with the fear of missing out, you got them with the 30 day action plan, you got them with the why you got them with, uh, the initial conversation, if they'd leave the, if they leave the office without having canceled, um, that is a recovery that person. Uh, and you should be at about 25 to 50% recovery and anybody, uh, wishing to cancel if they follow this exact process. Um, if you cannot give them to, if you cannot get them to agree to anything that we covered, um, you've done everything that you can and you've given it a good amount of effort, like, so don't feel bad about that. Like you can't save everybody. Right. But if you went through this whole process, you did it great. Um, that's, that's what you should be doing for retention. You didn't just send them a form and ask them to fill it out, which is probably what most gym owners, like when they first started business is what they did. Right. So some people cannot be recovered. So it's best to just leave on good terms, right? Say, well, I hope, you know, I really hope that you learned something. I really hope that we, uh, helped you improve your life in some way, or you created some new habits that you didn't have before. Um, and just leave on really good terms. Cause it's very likely that if you offered a great service, they might be back. Right. If your services is honestly not that good, then they probably won't be back. But if you, if you really do believe in your service and you really do think that, um, you're unique and that you're offering something great, the chances are that they'll be back. So leave on the best terms possible. Okay. So now we are going to dig into the exit interview form. It is super simple. It's super easy. Let's scroll to the top here. Um, this is mostly when you, when you have this form in front of you, I always have it written, like, of course, you can make it on the computer or you can use Google forms or something like that. Do it fancier, but people like there is nothing better than just some pen and some paper and a real conversation when it comes to this kind of stuff. So this is not meant to be filled out meticulously long where you're staring at the paper. It's just there to help guide you through the conversations. So you don't forget to talk about anything. Right. Um, mostly you're not going to be looking at the paper. You're just going to be looking at them and you're actually having a conversation with them. The goal is for you to know the four points, um, of the conversation that you want to reach. And this is just the place for you to take notes, but you want to look at them and talk about those four points, um, and not be staring at your paper, looking like you're filling out a form at the DMV. You want to have a real conversation. So, um, pretty simple. We start off with a reminder. This is for yourself too. This, like this is an, a cancellation form that they would fill out. This is, this is internal. This is just for you. So we put at the top, do not send them this Google form. Um, this must be an in-person or zoom, uh, meeting, right? So this is, uh, you want to be face to face talking with them. Right. Then you fill out the client, the name, the date, um, the coach. This is just you. I mean, or if you have a coach to exit interviews, whoever's conducting the interview, right? And then we want to write down the reason, uh, for their cancellation. Why, what brought you in here? Like, why do you want to cancel? That gives you a great place to start, um, asking questions. Then as you go down, uh, we cover the objectives of the exit interview, reconnect with the client. That's the first thing that we talked about. Um, we want to, uh, refocus on the client and why they started. We want to, uh, hopefully save the client with a short-term action plans to revisit in 30 days. And then we go straight into the, uh, questions because fear of missing out, FOMO comes after the questions. You don't want to like make it seem like you're bullying them before you even ask them questions as to why they're canceling. So here is the questions that we just went over, uh, but it's in written form. So you don't forget, um, why are you wanting to cancel? What goals do you have in mind when you join the gym? Um, did you achieve that goal with us? Is it still a goal of yours? Um, where were we able to help you? Like, where did we add value to your life and what could we have done better? Like, where did we fail you? Right. And then the final thing is like, what can we do now? What can we do to keep you as part of the family? Like, what would it take? Right. And just see what they have to say. Um, after you answer those pretty important questions, that's when you get into FOMO, if you didn't recover them and say, like, Hey, look, if they cancel, um, rejoining it with the same coaches and guaranteed, you might be grandfathered right, that you can't rejoin it at a later time. Then it's like the next question is, let's create a 30 day action plan right now and revisit this conversation in 30 days. Right. We set goals, kick off a nutrition, uh, plan and start tracking. Does that sound good? And that's like a very straightforward question that you asked them. Um, and hopefully they say, yeah, you know what, let's do it. That's set a goal. We're, this is what I want to do here. I want to lose 10 pounds, whatever. Um, if they say yes, then you create the plan right then and there to help them reach the goal that they mentioned above and follow up, set a follow up appointment in 30 days, set the appointment that would, that will make them not want to cancel before that appointment. Very important to do that. If you say, Hey, I'll reach out to you in 30 days, um, and we'll set a follow up appointment. They're not going to give it 110% or they, they, the chance of them giving 110% isn't as high because they can reach out to you at any point and be like, you know what, I thought about it and I really, but if you have an appointment set up there, like, okay, May 28th is when I meet with Joey again. You know, I'm going to give it 110% until May 28th. And then I'll see that hard deadline, um, really helps increase their effort. Right. Um, however, if they say no, then you were not able to recover them. Wish them the best of luck and let them know that you'll cancel their, uh, you'll process their cancellation shortly, however you do it, however you do cancellations, right? So this is the entire process that you'll go through. Um, when it comes to somebody saying, I would like to cancel, as you can see, it's probably more robust than what you're used to doing, which is why it works. Right. So whether it's you doing it or your staff, don't let anybody, uh, get through canceling without going through this and you will recover some. So hopefully you guys learned a lot, um, during this lesson and now, uh, we can take this and jump right into the next.
